title: What is an LSTM?
concept: LSTMs solve vanishing gradients in RNNs by introducing a cell state (a conveyor belt) alongside the hidden state, letting information flow unchanged.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary limitation of standard Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)?

options.text: They tend to forget early information in long sequences.
They are too fast and skip inputs.
They can only process one word at a time forever.

exercises.explain: Standard RNNs struggle with long sequences because gradients vanish (fade to zero) during training, making them forget early inputs.

cards.title: Memory Under Pressure
cards.body: Have you ever tried to memorize a long story, but forgot the beginning by the time you reached the end? Standard Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) suffer from the same problem—they have short-term memory loss! Fortunately, LSTMs were invented to help AI remember long stories perfectly.

cards.title: Meet the LSTM
cards.body: An LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) is a special kind of Recurrent Neural Network designed to remember information for long periods. It introduces a "cell state" (an internal memory highway) and "gates" that decide what information to add, keep, or throw away.

cards.title: The Conveyor Belt Analogy
cards.body: Think of a standard RNN like a game of telephone where players whisper a message down a long line; the message quickly gets garbled or forgotten. An LSTM is like having a conveyor belt running alongside the line of players. If a player has an important note, they can just place it on the conveyor belt to slide directly to the end of the line without being changed or lost!

cards.title: Keeping Track of the Plot
cards.body: Imagine reading a long book. In chapter 1, it mentions 'Alice lives in Paris'. A standard RNN might forget this detail by chapter 10 because too many sentences have passed. An LSTM uses its cell state to keep 'Paris' safe in memory, so it can correctly answer a question about her location at the very end of the book.

cards.title: The Name Trap
cards.body: You might think that because LSTMs have 'short-term' in their name, they cannot remember things for long. Actually, the name means they create a 'long-lasting' form of 'short-term memory' (activation memory), allowing them to link clues across thousands of steps.

cards.title: RNN vs. LSTM Memory
cards.body: Comparing how standard RNNs and LSTMs handle sequential data over many steps.

right.text: Cell state preserves information over long gaps, regulated by memory gates.

left.text: Information fades quickly; struggles to connect early clues to the final output.

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of the "cell state" in an LSTM?

options.text: It deletes all past information at every single step.
It carries key information directly through time like a conveyor belt.
It converts text into images so they are easier to store.
It increases the speed of the network by skipping hidden layers.

exercises.explain: The cell state acts like a continuous highway or conveyor belt, allowing crucial details to flow through many steps without fading away.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Standard RNNs struggle with long-term memory because gradients fade away during training over long sequences.
exercises.explain: Standard RNNs struggle to carry gradients over long sequences, causing them to forget early inputs. LSTMs were specifically designed to solve this issue.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence describing how LSTMs control their memory.
exercises.sentence: An LSTM regulates its memory by opening and closing special ______ that filter information.
exercises.explain: LSTMs use structures called gates to decide which information should be kept, updated, or erased from their internal memory.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI agent that represents an LSTM.

options.text: I process sequences step-by-step, but my memory naturally fades fast because I don't have a cell state or gates to protect it.
I use a cell state as an internal memory highway, and gates to control what is remembered, preventing the vanishing gradient problem.
I don't look at sequences at all; I just process independent inputs one at a time and forget them immediately.

exercises.explain: AI Model B correctly describes the unique feature of LSTMs: using a cell state (conveyor belt) and gates to preserve long-term context. Model A describes a standard RNN, and Model C describes a simple feedforward network.

exercises.prompt: How do LSTMs successfully prevent the vanishing gradient problem that plagues traditional RNNs?

options.text: They completely remove the hidden state to save memory.
They use a cell state highway and gates to protect old information.
They stop using backpropagation so gradients never change.
They only read the very last word of any text input.

exercises.explain: RNNs struggle to hold onto information over long gaps because gradients shrink to zero during training (vanishing gradient). LSTMs solve this by using a cell state as a continuous highway, managed by gates that control what to keep or discard.
